title: Intelligent Optimal Control of Rydberg Gates with Incremental-Update Deep Reinforcement Learning
authors: Yue Cai, Hanlin Zhang, Keye Zhang, Jing Qian
abstract: Deep reinforcement learning (DRL), acting as a novel and powerful paradigm for quantum optimal control, offers transformative opportunities for advancing neutral-atom quantum computing. In this work, we theoretically demonstrate a DRL-based framework for realizing Rydberg controlled-NOT gates that achieve both high speed and high fidelity through the synchronous modulation of multiple pulse parameters without any prior heuristic ansatz. By introducing an incremental-update learning policy, our framework effectively regularizes the exploration of the control landscape, ensuring the generation of smooth, experimentally feasible pulse profiles while significantly reducing computational overhead compared to conventional schemes. Crucially, the framework autonomously discovers an early-cutoff policy by optimally reconciling operation speed with high-precision coherent control. Our optimized protocol achieves a peak average fidelity of 0.9991, significantly outperforming conventional methods and surpassing the critical fault-tolerant threshold. This work establishes a generalizable, AI-driven pathway for designing high-performance quantum gates and provides a robust paradigm for autonomous control field optimization across diverse qubit platforms.
summary: This paper introduces an incremental-update deep reinforcement learning framework to optimize Rydberg-based CNOT gates. By simultaneously modulating pulse amplitudes and phases, the method achieves extremely high fidelities and significantly faster gate operations than traditional methods. The approach is particularly notable for its ability to autonomously discover efficient, non-intuitive control strategies that are robust to experimental noise.
main_problem: Designing high-fidelity, high-speed Rydberg CNOT gates in neutral-atom arrays while overcoming the fidelity-speed trade-off and the limitations of traditional piecewise adiabatic protocols.
main_result: The proposed IU-DRL framework achieves a peak average gate fidelity of 0.9991 and reduces gate duration by 38.9% compared to conventional methods, while remaining robust against thermal motion.
method: Incremental-Update Deep Reinforcement Learning (IU-DRL) using a Trust Region Policy Optimization (TRPO) architecture to synchronously modulate pulse amplitudes and phases.
model_or_system: A system of two trapped 87Rb atoms modeled as four-level systems interacting via van der Waals forces, including dynamics for spontaneous decay and atomic thermal motion.
key_observables: Average gate fidelity (F_avg), gate duration, and population leakage into Rydberg and intermediate states.
important_parameters: Rydberg interaction strength V0 = 2pi x 450 MHz, temperature T = 10 uK, and smoothing coefficients xi = 0.1.
main_assumptions: The primary error source in the control atom is assumed to be spontaneous decay during its residency in the Rydberg state.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the DRL cycle and optimized pulse profiles; Figure 3 illustrates the learning of the early-cutoff strategy; Figure 4 compares the proposed method against suboptimal traditional update methods; Figure 6 shows population dynamics and pulse sequences for different smoothing regimes.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of Rydberg gate optimization, describes the IU-DRL framework and its incremental update mechanism, presents results on fidelity and speedup, demonstrates robustness against thermal noise, and compares the performance against traditional adiabatic and non-adiabatic benchmarks.
why_interesting: It presents a novel AI-driven approach to control complex, non-adiabatic dynamics in Rydberg systems, offering a way to bypass the performance ceilings of traditional adiabatic protocols in neutral-atom quantum computing.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Implementation of Rydberg gates via the IU-DRL intelligent optimization. (a) In the DRL cycle, agent receives the state information ⃗S(ti) from environment at every time step ti and determines the action ⃗A(ti) according to the learned policy. Meanwhile, the policy is iteratively updated under the guidance of reward signal R(ti). Then the environment evolves over the next time step based on the selected action, yielding updated state ⃗S(ti+1) and updated reward R(ti+1), which are fed back to the agent as inputs for the ti+1 time step. (b) The synchronously-optimized pulse amplitudes (Ωc(t), Ωt(t)) and phases (ϕc(t), ϕt(t)) for a given duration tN = 0.4µs with N = 100, which are...

FIG. 3. Evolution of IU-DRL-optimized pulse amplitudes and phases across different training stages. The synchronous-pulse protocol follows the setup in Sec. III A but with increased smoothing coeﬀicients (ξΩ, ξϕ) = 0.3. Panels from top to bottom correspond to Cases II–IV in Table I, representing the results after (a1-2) 25,000, (b1-2) 50,000, and (c1-2) 75,000 training epochs, respectively. All other physical parameters are identical to those in Fig. 1b.

FIG. 4. Suboptimal control strategies identified by the DRL agent utilizing the traditional update (TU) method. Panels (a)–(d) and (e)–(h) illustrate two distinct classes of solutions that emerge stochastically as the number of training epochs increases. The first class [(a)–(d)] is analogous to a piecewise-EIT protocol, characterized by the deactivation of the control- atom laser (Ωc(t) ≈0) during the target-atom operation. The second class [(e)–(h)] exhibits a synchronous-like profile where Ωc(t) remains near its maximum throughout the sequence, yet fails to incorporate the refined early-cutoff mechanism. Detailed gate parameters for these cases are summarized in Table I (Cases V–VIII)....

FIG. 5. Impact of atomic thermal motion on gate infi- delity δF for the synchronously-optimized IU-DRL protocol I (red squares) and the traditional piecewise adiabatic scheme I (blue triangles). The total infidelity is decomposed into (a) Doppler shift error from random thermal velocities and (b) Rydberg interatomic interaction error from position fluctua- tions in the optical trap (ω/2π = 100 kHz). The synchronous protocol exhibits superior robustness against Doppler-induced dephasing due to its significantly shorter gate duration. In contrast, the interaction error in (b) remains negligible (< 10−7) for both schemes, as the gate operates deep within the strong Rydberg blockade regime.
